questions.question: What is the C-type's primary fear in a buying decision?

options.text: Missing out on the latest trend
Upsetting their coworkers
Being wrong or making a mistake based on bad data
Losing control of the conversation

questions.explanation: C-types are perfectionists. They fear criticism. Making a bad purchase decision means they failed to analyze the situation strictly enough.

questions.question: A prospect asks a highly technical question you do not know the answer to. What is the best response for a C-type?

options.text: Make a best guess to keep the conversation moving
Say 'That's not important right now'
Admit you don't know and promise to follow up with the exact answer
Change the subject to pricing

questions.explanation: C-types value accuracy above all. If you guess and are wrong, you lose all trust. Admitting ignorance but promising accuracy builds trust.

questions.question: Which word should you avoid when selling to a C-type?

options.text: Specific
Proven
Revolutionary
Documented

questions.explanation: 'Revolutionary' sounds like marketing hype. C-types distrust hype. They prefer specific, proven, documented facts.

questions.question: Why can 'Founder selling to Technical Architect' (C to C) be dangerous?

options.text: They will talk forever about technology and forget to sell the business value
They will hate each other immediately
The founder will not understand the architect
The architect will ask for a job

questions.explanation: When two C-types meet, they can easily fall into an 'Analysis Loop' or a technical debate, ignoring the commercial aspect of the deal.

questions.question: What is the ideal 'Next Step' to offer a C-type?

options.text: A contract to sign immediately
A happy hour with the team
Sending over technical documentation or a whitepaper for review
A ballpark pricing estimate

questions.explanation: C-types need to 'do their homework.' Giving them documentation gives them the control to analyze and verify your claims before committing.
